title: Destination Dispatch, Explained
text: Conventional elevator dispatch assigns a car to a hall call only after a passenger presses an up or down button and boards, leaving the car itself to learn the passenger's actual floor once the passenger presses a destination button inside the cabin. Ascentra instead asks a passenger to enter their destination floor at a kiosk before boarding, and assigns that passenger to a specific car immediately, grouping passengers headed to nearby floors onto the same car before it ever leaves the lobby. This upfront grouping is the single mechanism responsible for destination dispatch's efficiency advantage over conventional call-and-respond systems, since a car serving passengers grouped by destination makes fewer intermediate stops than a car accepting passengers grouped only by direction. Ascentra first shipped commercially in 2017 and now runs in office towers, hospitals, and mixed-use high-rises across several hundred buildings.

title: Hall-Call Assignment Logic
text: When a passenger enters a destination at an Ascentra kiosk, the dispatch engine evaluates every available car's current position, direction, and already-assigned passenger list, estimating the additional travel time each car would incur if assigned this new passenger. The engine assigns the passenger to whichever car minimizes a weighted combination of that passenger's own expected wait and travel time and the added delay imposed on passengers already assigned to the chosen car. This weighting is deliberate: a naive assignment minimizing only the new passenger's own wait time tends to overload whichever car happens to be nearest, degrading service for passengers already aboard. Ascentra recomputes this assignment in under 40 milliseconds on typical hardware, fast enough that a passenger sees their assigned car's letter or number displayed at the kiosk essentially instantly after entering a destination.

title: Up-Peak and Down-Peak Traffic Patterns
text: Elevator traffic in a typical office high-rise follows a strongly asymmetric daily pattern: up-peak, concentrated in the morning as arriving employees travel from the lobby to their floors, and down-peak, concentrated in the evening as the building empties. Ascentra detects the onset of each pattern by monitoring the ratio of lobby-originating to non-lobby-originating destination requests over a rolling window, switching dispatch strategy once that ratio crosses a configurable threshold rather than relying on a fixed time-of-day schedule that would perform poorly on days with unusual traffic. During detected up-peak, Ascentra biases car assignment toward keeping cars near the lobby rather than dispatching them to serve isolated mid-building requests, since lobby-originating demand dominates and a car sent away from the lobby during up-peak often returns to find a large accumulated queue.

title: Wait-Time and Travel-Time Benchmarks
text: Ascentra's reference deployment, a 52-story mixed-use tower instrumented for continuous performance monitoring, recorded a median passenger wait time of 18.4 seconds from destination entry to car arrival during typical daytime operation, and 31.2 seconds during detected up-peak conditions. Average time-to-destination, measured from car arrival to passenger disembarkation, was 71 seconds across the same deployment. Under the previous conventional dispatch system the tower operated before switching to Ascentra in 2021, median up-peak wait time measured 46.8 seconds, meaning Ascentra's destination-grouping approach reduced up-peak wait time by roughly 33 percent. The tower's 8 elevator cars handle a combined average of 14,200 passenger trips on a typical weekday.

title: Kiosk Hardware and Uptime
text: Ascentra's destination-entry kiosks run on a dedicated embedded controller rated for continuous operation, with a mean time between failures of 4.1 years across the installed base as of the most recent hardware reliability report. Kiosk touchscreen response latency averages 60 milliseconds, and the kiosk-to-dispatch-engine round trip, from destination entry to car assignment being finalized, averages 38 milliseconds over the building's local network. Ascentra's reference deployment operates 14 kiosks across its lobby and sky-lobby transfer floors, each capable of queuing up to 6 pending assignments simultaneously during brief processing delays. Kiosk uptime across the installed base measured 99.94 percent over the trailing 12 months, with the majority of recorded downtime attributable to scheduled firmware updates rather than unplanned hardware failure.

title: Car Capacity and Load Sensing
text: Each Ascentra-managed car carries a load cell beneath its cabin floor, reporting total passenger weight to the dispatch engine continuously rather than only at door-close. Cars in Ascentra's reference deployment carry a rated capacity of 2,041 kilograms, roughly 26 passengers at the building code's assumed average passenger weight, though the dispatch engine begins declining new assignments once estimated load reaches 85 percent of rated capacity, reserving headroom for passengers already assigned but not yet boarded. Load-sensing accuracy is rated at plus or minus 2 percent of rated capacity, sufficient to detect a car approaching its practical passenger limit well before the door-obstruction sensor or overweight alarm would otherwise trigger a boarding refusal.

title: Deployment Scale and Version History
text: Ascentra version 4.1, the current production release as of mid-2026, is deployed across an estimated 340 buildings spanning 2,900 individual elevator cars, according to the vendor's most recent installed-base disclosure. The largest single deployment, a 72-story mixed-use tower, coordinates 22 cars across three separate zoned banks under a single Ascentra dispatch instance. Version 3.0, released in 2020, was the first release to support cross-bank transfer coordination for buildings using sky lobbies, and version 4.0, released in 2023, added the up-peak and down-peak pattern-detection logic described elsewhere in this documentation, replacing an earlier fixed-schedule approach that had shipped since the platform's original 2017 release.

title: Energy Consumption Impact
text: Destination-grouped dispatch reduces total car travel distance compared to conventional call-and-respond dispatch, since fewer intermediate stops directly translates into less acceleration, deceleration, and door-cycling energy per passenger trip. Ascentra's reference deployment measured a 21 percent reduction in aggregate elevator energy consumption after switching from conventional dispatch in 2021, from a baseline of 340 kilowatt-hours per day across the tower's 8 cars to 269 kilowatt-hours per day under otherwise comparable traffic conditions. Regenerative braking, which several of the tower's cars use to recover energy during downward travel with sufficient counterweight imbalance, contributed an additional measured 6 percent reduction beyond the dispatch-algorithm improvement alone, though this contribution depends on car hardware rather than anything Ascentra's dispatch logic controls directly.

title: Wait Time by Traffic Pattern
text:
Ascentra's reference deployment tracks median and 95th-percentile wait time separately across its three detected traffic patterns, using continuous kiosk-to-arrival timestamps collected over a full calendar quarter of typical daily operation.

| Traffic pattern | Median wait | P95 wait |
|---|---|---|
| Up-peak | 31.2s | 58.4s |
| Down-peak | 26.7s | 49.1s |
| Inter-floor (typical) | 18.4s | 34.9s |

Up-peak consistently shows the widest gap between median and P95 wait time of the three measured patterns, reflecting how quickly a temporary local surge in lobby demand can outpace even well-optimized car availability during the morning arrival window.

title: Dispatch Algorithm Comparison
text:
Ascentra's internal benchmarking suite compares its default dispatch algorithm against two alternative strategies on identical simulated traffic representative of a typical 52-story mixed-use tower during a full business day.

| Strategy | Median wait | Avg trips per car-hour | Energy per trip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ascentra default | 18.4s | 142 | 100% (baseline) |
| Nearest-car greedy | 24.1s | 119 | 118% |
| Fixed-zone sectoring | 21.9s | 127 | 109% |

Nearest-car greedy assignment performs worst on every measured column despite its intuitive simplicity, since it systematically ignores the added delay imposed on passengers already assigned to whichever car happens to be nearest a new request.

title: Assignment Cost Function and Complexity Bound
text: Ascentra's assignment engine minimizes a cost function C = w1 * T_wait_new + w2 * sum(delta_T_existing), where T_wait_new is the new passenger's estimated wait time, delta_T_existing is the added delay imposed on each already-assigned passenger in the candidate car, and w1, w2 are tunable weights set to 0.6 and 0.4 respectively in the default configuration. Evaluating this cost function exactly across all cars and all possible reassignment permutations would require time exponential in passenger count, so Ascentra instead evaluates only the incremental cost of inserting the new passenger into each car's existing route without permuting already-assigned stops, an O(n log n) approximation per car where n is that car's current passenger count. This approximation occasionally produces a slightly suboptimal assignment compared to full re-optimization, but keeps assignment latency low enough for real-time kiosk response.

title: International Research on Elevator Traffic Modeling
text: Elevator dispatch research draws on traffic-modeling traditions published across several language communities beyond the English-language vertical-transportation literature most engineers encounter first. Nakagawa and Endo's 2016 study, 目的地予約方式における群管理 アルゴリズムの評価, established much of the destination-grouping evaluation methodology Ascentra's own internal benchmarking suite follows. Their finding that grouping passengers by destination floor before dispatch, rather than only by direction, produced measurably fewer intermediate stops under simulated high-rise traffic directly informed Ascentra's original 2017 architecture. Ascentra's technical documentation cites the study directly in its dispatch-algorithm design rationale, alongside more commonly referenced North American vertical-transportation handbooks.

title: European Sky-Lobby Transfer Standards
text: Ascentra's cross-bank transfer coordination, added in version 3.0, drew heavily on a 2019 German-language reference, Verkehrsmanagement bei Sky-Lobby-Systemen in Hochhäusern, examining how several European high-rise projects coordinated passenger transfers between express and local elevator banks sharing a common sky lobby. The paper's finding that transfer-floor congestion, rather than either individual bank's own dispatch efficiency, was the dominant bottleneck in several studied buildings shaped Ascentra's decision to treat sky-lobby transfer capacity as an explicit constraint in its cross-bank assignment logic rather than optimizing each bank independently and assuming transfers would resolve themselves. Ascentra's version 3.0 release notes credit the paper directly when describing the cross-bank coordination feature's design rationale.

title: Kiosk Commissioning Checklist
text:
Technicians follow this sequence when commissioning a new Ascentra destination-entry kiosk during building installation or a hardware replacement.
1. Confirm the kiosk's network link to the dispatch engine reports round-trip latency under 100 milliseconds.
2. Verify the kiosk's floor-selection interface matches the building's actual floor numbering, including any lobby or mezzanine offsets.
3. Run a test assignment sequence and confirm the correct car letter or number displays within 2 seconds.
4. Calibrate the touchscreen and confirm response latency stays under the 100 millisecond target across all active zones.
5. Register the kiosk's serial number against the building's dispatch-engine configuration for maintenance tracking.
Kiosks failing step 2 are the most common commissioning failure, typically traced to a floor-numbering configuration error rather than a hardware defect.

title: Up-Peak Response Escalation Procedure
text:
When Ascentra's pattern-detection logic identifies an up-peak surge exceeding normal capacity, building operations staff follow this escalation procedure.
1. Confirm the dispatch engine has correctly switched into up-peak mode by checking the operations dashboard.
2. Verify all available cars are participating in dispatch, since a car removed for maintenance during up-peak significantly worsens wait times.
3. Check queue length at each active kiosk for evidence of a localized bottleneck rather than building-wide surge.
4. Open a currently idle service car to general passenger use if queue length exceeds the configured escalation threshold.
5. Log the escalation event for inclusion in the weekly traffic-pattern review.
Step 4 is used sparingly, since pulling a service car into general dispatch reduces capacity available for freight and maintenance tasks scheduled later that day.

title: FAQ: Load-Sensing Threshold
text:
Q: At what load percentage does Ascentra begin declining new passenger assignments to a car?
A: Cars in Ascentra's reference deployment carry a rated capacity of 2,041 kilograms, roughly 26 passengers at the building code's assumed average passenger weight, though the dispatch engine begins declining new assignments once estimated load reaches 92 percent of rated capacity, reserving headroom for passengers already assigned but not yet boarded. Building operators ask this question frequently during commissioning, since a threshold set too low wastes car capacity while one set too high risks overcrowding complaints from passengers boarding a car that appears full. The threshold is configurable per building and is typically tuned during the first few weeks of live operation based on observed passenger behavior and any overcrowding feedback collected through the building's facilities team.

title: Corrigendum: Reference Deployment Energy Figures
text: This corrigendum corrects the switchover date attached to an energy-consumption figure published in an earlier revision of Ascentra's reference-deployment case study, following an internal data review conducted ahead of the platform's most recent sustainability report. The corrected passage reads: Ascentra's reference deployment measured a 21 percent reduction in aggregate elevator energy consumption after switching from conventional dispatch in 2019, from a baseline of 340 kilowatt-hours per day across the tower's 8 cars to 269 kilowatt-hours per day under otherwise comparable traffic conditions. The original case study's switchover date was transcribed incorrectly from an internal commissioning record during the document's first drafting pass, an error that went unnoticed through two subsequent editorial reviews before being caught during the sustainability report's independent data verification process. The corrected figure now appears in all current published versions of the case study.

title: History: From Fixed Scheduling to Adaptive Pattern Detection
text: Ascentra's dispatch engine began in 2017 with a single, relatively simple assignment algorithm and a fixed daily schedule dividing building operation into morning up-peak, evening down-peak, and ordinary inter-floor windows based purely on time of day, an approach that mirrored how many conventional elevator systems of the era already handled traffic pattern switching. This fixed schedule worked reasonably well for buildings with predictable, office-standard occupancy hours, but performed poorly for mixed-use towers combining office floors with residential or hospitality floors following entirely different daily rhythms, since a single building-wide schedule could not simultaneously serve both populations well. Early mixed-use deployments during 2018 and 2019 surfaced this limitation repeatedly, with building operators reporting that the fixed evening down-peak window frequently activated hours before or after actual demand shifted, degrading service during the mismatch period. The team's first response was allowing operators to manually configure custom schedules per building, which helped but still required someone to notice and correct a schedule that no longer matched a building's evolving occupancy pattern, itself a maintenance burden many building operations teams were not equipped to handle proactively. The real fix, adaptive pattern detection based on live traffic ratios rather than any fixed schedule, was designed over the following two years and shipped in version 4.0 in 2023, replacing time-of-day switching entirely with continuous monitoring of the ratio between lobby-originating and non-lobby-originating destination requests. This change required substantially more real-time computation than the simple schedule lookup it replaced, but by 2023 the dispatch engine's underlying hardware had enough headroom that the added computation posed no practical latency concern for kiosk response times. Adaptive detection immediately improved wait-time metrics across every mixed-use deployment in the installed base without requiring any building-specific manual tuning, essentially eliminating the operator-configuration burden the interim manual-schedule feature had only partially addressed. Looking back, the team describes the move from fixed scheduling to adaptive detection as the single change most responsible for Ascentra's expansion beyond conventional office towers into the mixed-use and hospitality markets that now represent a meaningful share of the installed base, markets the original fixed-schedule architecture had never served particularly well. The team also notes a quieter lesson from the transition: the interim manual-schedule feature, though eventually superseded, was not wasted effort, since it surfaced which operator-facing controls building staff actually wanted to adjust versus which they consistently left at default values and expected the system to get right on its own. That distinction shaped which parameters adaptive detection exposed as configurable settings and which it made fully automatic, a choice the team credits with keeping version 4.0's configuration surface far smaller than it otherwise might have become.

title: Incident Postmortem: The 2024 Sky-Lobby Transfer Bottleneck
text: In September 2024, a 72-story mixed-use tower running Ascentra 4.0 experienced a sustained transfer-floor bottleneck during three consecutive weekday up-peak periods, with passenger complaints concentrated among tenants on floors served only by the building's upper local bank, reachable exclusively via a sky-lobby transfer from the express bank connecting the ground floor. Measured wait time at the sky-lobby transfer kiosks reached a median of 94 seconds during the affected mornings, more than triple the building's typical up-peak median, despite the express bank itself showing no unusual delay from the ground floor to the sky lobby. Investigation found the cross-bank coordination logic, which treats sky-lobby transfer capacity as an explicit constraint in assignment decisions, had been configured during the tower's original commissioning with a transfer-capacity assumption based on the building's pre-occupancy projected tenant mix rather than its actual, since-changed occupancy, which had shifted toward a higher concentration of upper-floor tenants than originally planned following a lease renegotiation completed after commissioning. The mismatch meant the dispatch engine was still allocating transfer capacity according to an outdated tenant distribution, systematically under-provisioning upper-local-bank transfers relative to actual demand even though the express bank itself had ample spare capacity to deliver more passengers to the sky lobby. The postmortem's central finding was that transfer-capacity assumptions, unlike traffic-pattern detection, were not automatically re-derived from live data, and had effectively become stale configuration nobody was monitoring for drift after initial commissioning. The immediate fix reconfigured the transfer-capacity assumption manually to match current occupancy, restoring median transfer wait time to 31 seconds within a day. The longer-term remediation, shipped in version 4.1, added periodic automatic recalibration of transfer-capacity assumptions based on observed transfer demand over a rolling 90-day window, removing the dependency on a one-time commissioning-era estimate that earlier versions had never revisited. The building's operations team also adopted a practice of flagging major leasing changes to Ascentra's support team going forward, so future occupancy shifts trigger a manual capacity review even before the automatic recalibration window would otherwise catch the drift. The postmortem also examined why three full days of degraded service passed before the pattern was correctly diagnosed, and found that the building's operations staff had initially attributed the elevated transfer wait times to ordinary up-peak variability rather than a configuration problem, since the express bank's own metrics looked entirely normal and nothing in the standard operations dashboard distinguished a capacity-assumption mismatch from routine demand fluctuation. Ascentra's support team has since added a dedicated transfer-capacity utilization panel to the operations dashboard, making a persistent mismatch between assumed and observed transfer demand visible well before it accumulates into the kind of sustained, multi-day bottleneck the tower experienced in September 2024.
